test_text.title: Conversation about Caring for Elderly Mother and Council Support

audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman who is caring for her elderly mother talking to a friend.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: It's really good to see you, Tom.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Since I had to give up work, I feel I'm losing touch with my friends and colleagues.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: We really miss you in the office.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: We were all so sorry you had to leave.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: But you must be relieved to have more time to look after your mother.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: How is she?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Well, she's very cheerful, but she needs a lot of help.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Have you tried to get any support from the local council?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: No, I didn't know I could.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Yes, they offer different kinds of practical support.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: They realize that carers sometimes need time for all the other responsibilities they have apart from the person they're caring for, and also that they sometimes need a break.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: uh Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Ok, so tell me more.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: How do I go about getting this support?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well, you'd have to have an assessment of your mother's needs.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: That means someone would come round and talk to you about the situation and what you need.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So for a start, they'd want to know the amount of time you spend looking after your mother every day.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Then they'll probably ask you what sorts of tasks you do for your mother during the day.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Things like if she needs help with getting dressed, for example.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Right, I help her with that, and also I help her get into the shower in the morning.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Yes, that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: They'll probably ask you if you do the shopping for her, and help her at meal times, and whether she can cope using money.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yes, that's becoming a bit of a problem.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: She used to be very good at it, but uh not anymore.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And be ready to tell them about anything you find particularly difficult about caring for your mother.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So recently, I've noticed she started to have quite bad problems with her memory.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: If I wasn't there, I think she'd forget to eat for example, and often she doesn't seem quite sure what day it is.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Yes, tell them about that.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And are there any physical difficulties you have caring for her?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Lifting her for example?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yes, she's quite heavy, and I'm afraid of hurting my back.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: I'd be in real trouble if that happened.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: They can give you advice about that, and also about how to avoid the possibility of your mom having a fall.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: So once they've done this assessment, if I'm eligible, uh what happens next?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well, they might support you financially, so they might help you with transport costs, like if you have to get a taxi to take your mother for an appointment for example.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I usually drive her myself, actually.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: So could I claim for the petrol?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: You could, and you can claim for the insurance too.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: And if you need help with the housework, they can arrange for someone to come along once or twice a week.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: And one other thing, I hope you don't mind me saying this, but it's important you look after yourself, and it seems to me you're under quite a bit of stress.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I am, yes.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Well tell the council, because they may be able to give you some advice on how to minimize it.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Though, actually, I feel so much better having talked to you.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: I'll get in touch with the council straight away.
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: Now, shall we go for coffee or some?
audioscript.speaker: CAROL

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Types of support that may be offered to carers

test_text.title: Elmleigh Town Council Volunteer Scheme Introduction

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man who works for Elmleigh Town Council talking to some people who want to join the small town's volunteer scheme.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: I'm Steve Wainwright from Elmleigh Town Council, and I organize the town's volunteer scheme.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: I'm delighted you're all interested in joining the scheme.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Our volunteers help to create a sense of community among the many people who live in our historic town of Elmleigh, and make residents and visitors feel welcome at local events.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: At first, I'll mention just a few of the activities that volunteers carry out.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: One is to walk around the town center streets wearing our volunteer T-shirt.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Tourists often ask how to get to a particular shop, and they might also be grateful for recommendations about what to visit.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The town holds a large number of concerts each year, and part of the volunteers' role is to get everyone in the audience to the right place as smoothly as possible.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: You'd be surprised how many people buy tickets then don't check them, and head for the wrong section of the hall.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Volunteers may get involved with community groups.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Such as sports clubs or gardeners' associations, here the volunteers talk about how groups can help each other.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: For instance, a writing group might want to travel to another town to hear a talk by a well known author, but may not know that another club has a coach they could travel in.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The town produces a monthly magazine, and anyone who lives in the town can send in articles.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: It's free to residents, and is paid for by local businesses.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: That's the responsibility of the council's advertising department.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: We depend on volunteers though, to find out what people think of events they've attended, and any suggestions they have for the future.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The volunteers then send a summary to the editors.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: There are a number of clubs for retired people, and every year, the council arranges lunch for all the members.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The volunteers welcome the guests.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: And when everyone's sitting down and relaxing after the meal, some volunteers put on a show, usually around half an hour of songs and short plays.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The club members really welcome the chance to chat to the volunteers at these events.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The town council has a website of course, and volunteers are asked to help by making sure residents know about it.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: It's updated every day with information about future activities.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: And we want as many people as possible to use it.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Ok, now you know some of the things our volunteers do.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: I'll go on to some practical matters.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: As you probably know, the town arranges three major festivals every year, and they all depend on a large number of volunteers.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: The Book Festival lasts three days.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: And uses several venues, which all need volunteers.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: More are needed for the music festival, because that lasts a whole week, and even more help is required for the science festival.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Even though it's only two days long, it involves quite a lot of venues though.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: It's a good idea to help at the festival you're most interested in, because you can attend most of the events for free.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: We try to use volunteers who are flexible though, because some festival events are held outdoors, and the weather may affect the size of the audience, and even whether the event can take place.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: So there can be changes at short notice.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: What's essential though, is being able to get on well with other people, and also to deal with someone who's behaving badly.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: As occasionally happens.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Our plan is to get you all working in September, after a week's training starting on the 2nd.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: So we'll be timetabling you for duties the following week from the 9th onward.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Later, in the week beginning September 23rd, uh we have a chat with each of you to find out how you feel about being a volunteer, and what extra support you need.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: As a thank you to the volunteers, we arrange an annual event.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: In recent years, we've had a party in the town hall, and last year, a barbecue in Chamber Park.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Our forthcoming event is a trip along the canal from here to Dewhurst and back.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: It's on Saturday, September the 28th, and if you'd like to attend, you can sign up once you start work.
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: Now, this is the volunteers' T-shirt...
audioscript.speaker: STEVE WAINWRIGHT

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part two.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What is the role of the volunteers in each of the following activities?
textReadable: What is the role of the volunteers in each of the following activities?

list: providing publicity about a council service
introducing guest speakers at an event
items: participating at lunches for retired people <input>
body.title: Activities

items.title: Which event requires the largest number of volunteers?
What is the most important requirement for volunteers at the festivals?
New volunteers will start working in the week beginning
What is the next annual event for volunteers?

test_text.title: Rosie and Colin's Discussion on Human Geography Assignment

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students called Rosie and Colin discussing their human Geography assignment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Colin, I'm really struggling to think of a topic for our human geography assignment.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Me too, Rosie.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I'll tell you what, let's think about the different aspects of human geography, and see if we can narrow the topic down a bit to help us decide.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: So one aspect is population. That would be all about population density and migration and so on, lots of facts and statistics, maybe a bit boring.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, but quite straightforward to find on the internet.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Suppose so, how about health?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I'd never thought about the links between that and geography until Professor Lee gave us that lecture on cholera.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: How in the 19th century, a physician used street plans and plans of water supplies to find the source of a cholera epidemic.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, fascinating, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Or we could do something more general like economies.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: So how financial and commercial factors are linked to the physical environment?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I thought that had been taken off the syllabus for this year.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Has it?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure, but it might be best to avoid it.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Ok, maybe we could do something on culture.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: We had that lecture about culture and geography last week.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I didn't get much out of that.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: It was all so general, and the lecturer didn't give any useful examples.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I hardly took any notes.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: It didn't seem worth it.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Me neither.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: We could focus on poverty, that's something that's a global problem.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: The trouble is, Doctor Lee was saying, that you have to be careful with some of the figures relating to poverty - they're sometimes deliberately manipulated.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: You mean the information gets changed for political reasons?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: That sort of thing, yes.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So what are we gonna do our assignment on?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I'll tell you what, one of the possibilities we haven't discussed is urbanization, and now over half the world's population lives in cities.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: That's really important.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Ok, good idea, rosie.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Let's do that.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I love living in a big city, but of course there are problems.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Things like theft and robbery.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yes, but where I come from that's linked to another more serious issue, which is that a lot of people don't have jobs.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: That's getting worse and worse, and we also still have quite a lot of people who are homeless, though that's not quite so bad as it was.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: It'd be nice to talk about some positive developments, uh like some of the new developments on the outskirts of cities.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yeah, they've opened some massive new shopping centers outside my city.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, the same with mine.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But it's meant a lot of the shops in the city center are closing down, but the outskirts are ideal for buildings that need a lot of space, like for conferences.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: They've opened a couple of big ones.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yes, we've got some too.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: It'd be nicer to have more facilities for things like football too, but that's not happening where I live.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Same in my area.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: We could include something about developing disused industrial sites.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: It seems like a good idea, because you're not doing any harm to the natural environment.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, but aren't the buildings architecturally significant?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Not really.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: And what people forget is that they often used quite dangerous materials, chemicals and things, which haven't been properly cleared away, so the whole site has to be made safe.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, that can't be cheap.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And I bet it's often not budgeted for.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: You're right.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Have you read about Masdar City?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: In Abu Dhabi?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, it was designed to be a green city, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: That might be a good example of a city which set out to depend entirely on renewable energy.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, we should say something about that.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: It was designed to be totally pedestrianized too, wasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: With the transport underground.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yes, and they had big plans for recycling to reduce waste to the lowest possible level.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: But let's stick to talking about power sources.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Then there's that ecotown in England, Greenhill Abbots.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: It set out to conform to the usual principles, sustainability and so on.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: A lot of people were against it at first.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: They said the plans were unrealistic.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure how far they've got with it.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: um I'll check.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Right, so it looks as if we have a sort of plan, and we can work from that.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What is Rosie and Colin's opinion about each of the following aspects of human geography?
textReadable: What is Rosie and Colin's opinion about each of the following aspects of human geography?

list: The information given about this was too vague.
This may not be relevant to their course.
This will involve only a small number of statistics.
It will be easy to find facts about this.
The facts about this may not be reliable.
No useful research has been done on this.
The information provided about this was interesting.
body.title: Aspects of human geography

items.title: Rosie says that in her own city the main problem is
What recent additions to the outskirts of their cities are both students happy about?
The students agree that developing disused industrial sites may
The students will mention Masdar City as an example of an attempt to achieve
When discussing the ecotown of Greenhill Abbots, Colin is uncertain about
options: why there was opposition to it.
how much of it has actually been built.

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a food science lecture on fashions in food.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: There are trends in food, much as there are trends in clothing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Interest in food fashions has risen rapidly since the birth of the smartphone when people first began taking photos of their food and instantly sharing them with their friends.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The food industry in the UK in particular is obsessed with finding and exploiting the next big food trend.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Marketers aim to create a huge demand for a food item which was previously unknown or not needed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the most effective ways of promoting a new food product is by using social media influencers as brand ambassadors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In return for free samples, many influencers will post content about a product.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although there are influencers with hundreds of thousands of followers who can command large fees for their services.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Companies which sell vegan produce were pioneers in being able to increase sales really quickly in this way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For a food item to become really popular, it has to be readily available.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So supermarkets have a role to play in creating a new food trend.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They have dedicated teams closely following which new products or ingredients are trending on social media, and are particularly interested in what well known chefs are putting on their menus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd like to look at a few examples of marketing campaigns which were really successful in launching a new fashion trend.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Starting in the 1990s with the avocado.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A British PR company was hired to raise its profile and stimulate demand.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They paid for a group of journalists to travel out to South Africa to meet avocado farmers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Articles written following this visit helped to educate the British public about the avocado.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which at this time was certainly not the daily staple it's since become.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Advertisements were designed to promote the avocado as a superfood, rich in nutrients, and therefore beneficial for health.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Avocados became hugely fashionable, and within a few years, UK avocado sales had grown from 13 million pounds annually.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To around 150 million, making it one of the most successful fresh produce campaigns in UK history.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Oat milk is a recent example of a new product which became fashionable very quickly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, there are many brands available.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But one company which had early success was the Swedish brand Oatly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They attracted a lot of attention with a media campaign which used provocation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a way of getting their message across effectively.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The fact that this campaign aggravated competitors producing milk from dairy cows was seen as a plus.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As it helped to make oat milk seem cool.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the USA, the brand decided against a big retail launch, in favor of getting the product into coffee chains, which removed the need for a big advertising budget.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This proved far more effective than offering samples in supermarkets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Oat milk had an advantage over other alternative milk products such as almond milk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many consumers prefer it, because it has less of an impact on the environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It requires significantly less water to produce than other alternative milk products, and it also has a relatively low carbon footprint.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Norwegian skrei, a rarely available seasonal fish delicacy, otherwise known as Arctic cod, is now found on the menus of Michelin starred restaurants throughout Europe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The demand for skrei has been used by a food marketing agency to build the reputation of Norway's fisheries in general.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Marketing surveys have shown that a significant number of shoppers now associate Norway with excellent seafood.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Food trends can be considered a good thing in some ways, as they can benefit farmers and food producers enormously.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The public can also be encouraged to buy things which are more sustainably produced, but ethical concerns have been raised about the effects a surge in demand can cause.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Quinoa is a classic example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This plant is native to Peru, and when demand peaked some years ago, the price soared.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Making it unaffordable for local people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While the popularity of quinoa has benefited farmers financially, there have been other negative consequences.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As demand grew, farmers began working the land all year round, in order to produce more quinoa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One issue has been that the fertility of the soil decreased dramatically, which could potentially lead to desertification in some areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another example would be the case of...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Many consumers prefer it, because it has less of an impact on the environment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It requires significantly less water to produce than other alternative milk products, and it also has a relatively low carbon footprint.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Norwegian skrei, a rarely available seasonal fish delicacy, otherwise known as Arctic cod, is now found on the menus of Michelin starred restaurants throughout Europe.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The demand for skrei has been used by a food marketing agency to build the reputation of Norway's fisheries in general.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Marketing surveys have shown that a significant number of shoppers now associate Norway with excellent seafood.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Food trends can be considered a good thing in some ways, as they can benefit farmers and food producers enormously.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The public can also be encouraged to buy things which are more sustainably produced, but ethical concerns have been raised about the effects a surge in demand can cause.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Quinoa is a classic example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This plant is native to Peru, and when demand peaked some years ago, the price soared.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Making it unaffordable for local people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: While the popularity of quinoa has benefited farmers financially, there have been other negative consequences.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As demand grew, farmers began working the land all year round, in order to produce more quinoa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One issue has been that the fertility of the soil decreased dramatically, which could potentially lead to desertification in some areas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another example would be the case of...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
